input.new_string:
# Plan — Annotated Error Reveal + Silent Identity Matching

## Context

Two improvements to SAGE's CLI learner experience, bundled because both sharpen the first few turns of a practice session.

### Part A — Output Evaluation Annotated Reveal

SAGE's **Output Evaluation** practice type currently presents an AI-generated output with embedded errors as a wall of plain text. The learner identifies errors in free-form conversation, and SAGE explains each error narratively from `groundTruth`. There is no visual scaffolding: no line references, no structured error taxonomy, and no way for SAGE to systematically re-show *where* each error lives after the learner answers.

The user wants to improve this by **revealing errors inline after the learner responds** — an annotated re-display of the AI output where each embedded error is marked with a numbered badge tied to a legend below (type, severity, explanation). This requires a **light schema extension**: an optional `errors[]` array on output-evaluation scenarios so SAGE has structured metadata to anchor the reveal.

Why this matters pedagogically:
- Learners currently finish the practice knowing only whether they "got it right." An annotated reveal lets them see *every* error (including the ones they missed) with precise location and a labeled error type.
- Named types (`hallucination`, `overstatement`, `logical_contradiction`, `missing_context`, `framing`) give learners a transferable vocabulary — one of SAGE's core rules ("Name principles explicitly so learners can transfer them").
- Schema-structured errors also unblock later work (difficulty tuning by severity, progress tracking per error type) without committing to a full taxonomy refactor today.

### Part B — Silent Identity Matching (No Name Prompt)

Today SAGE's session bootstrap ([.claude/skills/session-start.md](.claude/skills/session-start.md)) depends on the learner self-identifying in their first message, and if no name is provided the flow becomes awkward — either onboarding stalls without a display name or the agent has to ask "what should I call you?" This is friction we can eliminate: the Claude Code session already injects `userEmail: cyril@yesslab.org` into the conversation context at start, and a matching `data/users/cyril.json` already exists.

The user wants the agent to **never prompt for a name**. Instead it should: (a) read the email from the memory context, (b) match it to an existing profile, (c) pick up with whatever skill is appropriate — session-router for a returning learner, or onboarding for a first-time one — with onboarding never asking "what's your name?" either. Onboarding derives a display name from the email prefix and lets the learner correct it only if they volunteer.

# Plan — Annotated Error Reveal for Output Evaluation

## Context

SAGE's **Output Evaluation** practice type currently presents an AI-generated output with embedded errors as a wall of plain text. The learner identifies errors in free-form conversation, and SAGE explains each error narratively from `groundTruth`. There is no visual scaffolding: no line references, no structured error taxonomy, and no way for SAGE to systematically re-show *where* each error lives after the learner answers.

The user wants to improve this by **revealing errors inline after the learner responds** — an annotated re-display of the AI output where each embedded error is marked with a numbered badge tied to a legend below (type, severity, explanation). This requires a **light schema extension**: an optional `errors[]` array on output-evaluation scenarios so SAGE has structured metadata to anchor the reveal.

Why this matters pedagogically:
- Learners currently finish the practice knowing only whether they "got it right." An annotated reveal lets them see *every* error (including the ones they missed) with precise location and a labeled error type.
- Named types (`hallucination`, `overstatement`, `logical_contradiction`, `missing_context`, `framing`) give learners a transferable vocabulary — one of SAGE's core rules ("Name principles explicitly so learners can transfer them").
- Schema-structured errors also unblock later work (difficulty tuning by severity, progress tracking per error type) without committing to a full taxonomy refactor today.

## Approach

Three changes, each small:

### 1. Extend the scenario schema (optional `errors[]`)

File: [data/schemas/scenario.schema.json](data/schemas/scenario.schema.json)

Under `setup.context`, allow the object form to include an `errors` array. Each error entry has:

```json
{
  "id": "e1",
  "quote": "exact substring from aiOutput",
  "lineStart": 2,
  "lineEnd": 2,
  "type": "hallucination",
  "severity": "high",
  "explanation": "Fabricated citation — no Mitchell 2023 study exists."
}
```

- `type` enum: `hallucination`, `overstatement`, `logical_contradiction`, `missing_context`, `bias`, `framing`, `overconfidence`. Reuses three values from the existing `flawType` enum ([scenario.schema.json:65-69](data/schemas/scenario.schema.json#L65-L69)) and adds four output-evaluation-specific types.
- `severity` enum: `low`, `medium`, `high`.
- `lineStart` / `lineEnd` are 1-indexed against a line-split of `aiOutput`.
- `quote` is an exact substring (used as a fallback when line math drifts after editing).
- The whole `errors` array is **optional** — existing `groundTruth`-only scenarios keep working without migration. The schema change is purely additive.

### 2. Backfill the existing scenario

File: [data/scenarios/output-evaluation-errors.json](data/scenarios/output-evaluation-errors.json)

Add an `errors` array under `setup.context` derived from the existing narrative in `groundTruth` ([line 29](data/scenarios/output-evaluation-errors.json#L29)). Five errors to encode:

| id | line(s) | type | severity | quote anchor |
|----|---------|------|----------|--------------|
| e1 | 3 | hallucination | high | "Dr. Sarah Mitchell … Journal of Adolescent Psychology" |
| e2 | 7 | logical_contradiction / overstatement | high | "directly causes depression in teenagers" |
| e3 | 9 | overconfidence | medium | "approximately 90 minutes" |
| e4 | 11 | framing | low | "Instagram was found to be the most harmful" (characterization imprecise) |
| e5 | 13 | missing_context | medium | "adding at least 8 more citations" (compounds fabrication risk) |

Keep `groundTruth` as-is — it stays the narrative companion to the structured list. No pt to delete it.

### 3. Update scenario-runner to render line-numbered output + annotated reveal

File: [.claude/skills/scenario-runner/SKILL.md](.claude/skills/scenario-runner/SKILL.md)

Two insertions, both scoped to OUTPUT EVALUATION only (other practice types unchanged):

**(a) Step 5 — Present output in a line-numbered fenced block** (amend [lines 50-56](.claude/skills/scenario-runner/SKILL.md#L50-L56) with an OUTPUT EVALUATION-specific rendering note):

> For OUTPUT EVALUATION, present `setup.context.aiOutput` as a fenced code block with 1-indexed line numbers, e.g.:
> ````
> ```aioutput
> 1  Here's my feedback on your essay:
> 2
> 3  1. Your thesis about social media increasing anxiety is well-supported. A 2023 study by Dr. Sarah Mitchell in the Journal of Adolescent Psychology found that teens who use social media more than 3 hours daily are 2.4 times more likely to report anxiety symptoms.
> ...
> ```
> ````
> This gives learners a stable reference frame ("line 3 — the Mitchell study…") without changing the source string.

**(b) Step 7 — After EXPLAIN, render the Annotated Reveal** (new sub-step appended after [line 84](.claude/skills/scenario-runner/SKILL.md#L84)):

> If the scenario has `setup.context.errors[]`, close the feedback with an **Annotated Reveal**: a markdown block that re-shows the AI output with `[⚠ E1]`-style inline markers at each error's line, followed by a legend that names the type, severity, and explanation for every error — including the ones the learner already caught and the ones they missed.
>
> Template:
> ```
> ### Annotated reveal
>
> > Line 3: "…Dr. Sarah Mitchell's 2019 study…" [⚠ E1]
> > Line 7: "…directly causes depression in teenagers." [⚠ E2]
>
> **E1** · hallucination · high — Fabricated citation. No such study exists.
> **E2** · overstatement · high — Correlation ≠ causation; causal claim is disputed.
> **E3** · overconfidence · medium — …
> ```
>
> Ordering rule: list what the learner **caught** first (with a brief affirmation), then what they **missed**. This preserves the scaffolding principle — acknowledge before expanding.

**(c) Rules section** ([lines 95-103](.claude/skills/scenario-runner/SKILL.md#L95-L103)): add one bullet — "For OUTPUT EVALUATION scenarios with `errors[]`, end feedback with an Annotated Reveal so every embedded error is named, not just the ones the learner caught."

## Files to modify

- [data/schemas/scenario.schema.json](data/schemas/scenario.schema.json) — add optional `errors[]` definition under `setup.context`
- [data/scenarios/output-evaluation-errors.json](data/scenarios/output-evaluation-errors.json) — backfill 5 structured error entries
- [.claude/skills/scenario-runner/SKILL.md](.claude/skills/scenario-runner/SKILL.md) — add line-numbered rendering note + Annotated Reveal step + one rule bullet
- [CHANGELOG.md](CHANGELOG.md) — append dated entry (per user memory rule for agent-code edits)

## Explicitly out of scope

- Unifying `flawType` (bad-agent-simulator) with the new `errors[].type` enum — would turn this into a "full taxonomy refactor" and was rejected in the scope question.
- Changing the learner's response scaffold. They still respond in free text.
- Any changes to [.claude/skills/skill-evaluator/](.claude/skills/skill-evaluator/) — it continues to score against `evaluationCriteria` as today; it can optionally read `errors[]` later but not now.
- Streamlit/web UI work. CLI markdown only.

## Verification

1. **Schema validity**: `python -c "import json, jsonschema; s=json.load(open('data/schemas/scenario.schema.json')); sc=json.load(open('data/scenarios/output-evaluation-errors.json')); jsonschema.validate(sc, s); print('ok')"` — confirms the backfilled scenario still validates.
2. **Tool round-trip**: invoke `load_scenario` from [sage/tools.py](sage/tools.py) on `scenario-output-evaluation-errors-001` and confirm `errors` is present in the returned object.
3. **End-to-end skill run**: in a fresh Claude Code session, trigger `/scenario-runner` with output evaluation and walk through the scenario. Confirm:
   - The AI output is presented as a fenced code block with line numbers.
   - After the learner's response, the Annotated Reveal block appears with `[⚠ E1]` style markers and a legend listing all 5 errors.
   - Caught errors appear first in the legend with a brief affirmation; missed errors appear after.
   - The closing reflection question (Step 9) still fires.
4. **Backwards compatibility**: re-run any scenario that lacks `errors[]` (none today, but the schema must still accept them) — validate with the same jsonschema call using a stripped-down version of the scenario.

The user wants to improve this by **revealing errors inline after the learner responds** — an annotated re-display of the AI output where each embedded error is marked with a numbered badge tied to a legend below (type, severity, explanation). This requires a **light schema extension**: an optional `errors[]` array on output-evaluation scenarios so SAGE has structured metadata to anchor the reveal.

Why this matters pedagogically:
- Learners currently finish the practice knowing only whether they "got it right." An annotated reveal lets them see *every* error (including the ones they missed) with precise location and a labeled error type.
- Named types (`hallucination`, `overstatement`, `logical_contradiction`, `missing_context`, `framing`) give learners a transferable vocabulary — one of SAGE's core rules ("Name principles explicitly so learners can transfer them").
- Schema-structured errors also unblock later work (difficulty tuning by severity, progress tracking per error type) without committing to a full taxonomy refactor today.

### Part B — Silent Identity Matching (No Name Prompt)

Today SAGE's session bootstrap ([.claude/skills/session-start.md](.claude/skills/session-start.md)) depends on the learner self-identifying in their first message, and if no name is provided the flow becomes awkward — either onboarding stalls without a display name or the agent has to ask "what should I call you?" This is friction we can eliminate: the Claude Code session already injects `userEmail: cyril@yesslab.org` into the conversation context at start, and a matching `data/users/cyril.json` already exists.

The user wants the agent to **never prompt for a name**. Instead it should: (a) read the email from the memory context, (b) match it to an existing profile, (c) pick up with whatever skill is appropriate — session-router for a returning learner, or onboarding for a first-time one — with onboarding never asking "what's your name?" either. Onboarding derives a display name from the email prefix and lets the learner correct it only if they volunteer.

## Approach

Two independent change sets. Part A is presentation work in scenario-runner; Part B is bootstrap work in session-start + tools.py + schema. They don't share code paths but share a CHANGELOG entry.

---

### Part A — Annotated Error Reveal

Three changes, each small:

### A1. Extend the scenario schema (optional `errors[]`)

File: [data/schemas/scenario.schema.json](data/schemas/scenario.schema.json)

Under `setup.context`, allow the object form to include an `errors` array. Each error entry has:

```json
{
  "id": "e1",
  "quote": "exact substring from aiOutput",
  "lineStart": 2,
  "lineEnd": 2,
  "type": "hallucination",
  "severity": "high",
  "explanation": "Fabricated citation — no Mitchell 2023 study exists."
}
```

- `type` enum: `hallucination`, `overstatement`, `logical_contradiction`, `missing_context`, `bias`, `framing`, `overconfidence`. Reuses three values from the existing `flawType` enum ([scenario.schema.json:65-69](data/schemas/scenario.schema.json#L65-L69)) and adds four output-evaluation-specific types.
- `severity` enum: `low`, `medium`, `high`.
- `lineStart` / `lineEnd` are 1-indexed against a line-split of `aiOutput`.
- `quote` is an exact substring (used as a fallback when line math drifts after editing).
- The whole `errors` array is **optional** — existing `groundTruth`-only scenarios keep working without migration. The schema change is purely additive.

### 2. Backfill the existing scenario

File: [data/scenarios/output-evaluation-errors.json](data/scenarios/output-evaluation-errors.json)

Add an `errors` array under `setup.context` derived from the existing narrative in `groundTruth` ([line 29](data/scenarios/output-evaluation-errors.json#L29)). Five errors to encode:

| e3 | 9 | overconfidence | medium | "approximately 90 minutes" |
| e4 | 11 | framing | low | "Instagram was found to be the most harmful" (characterization imprecise) |

### Part B — Silent Identity Matching

Four small changes. The bootstrap flow becomes: read `userEmail` from injected memory context → try `load_user_profile(email=...)` → if hit, route straight to session-router / resume; if miss, route straight to onboarding without asking for a name.

### B1. Add `email` to the user-profile schema

File: [data/schemas/user-profile.schema.json](data/schemas/user-profile.schema.json)

Add an optional `email` string field under `properties`. Do **not** add it to `required` — existing profiles without email keep working. This field is the anchor for auto-match.

### B2. Backfill the existing Cyril profile with email

File: [data/users/cyril.json](data/users/cyril.json)

Add `"email": "cyril@yesslab.org"` so the memory-context email matches. No other profile needs backfilling — the `practitioner-researcher.json` and other test fixtures are anonymous and stay that way.

### B3. Extend `load_user_profile` to accept `email`

File: [sage/tools.py:36-58](sage/tools.py#L36-L58)

Change the signature from `load_user_profile(name: str)` to `load_user_profile(name: str = "", email: str = "")` with either-or semantics. When `email` is provided, do an exact (case-insensitive) match on the `email` field across all `data/users/*.json` files before falling back to the name-based scan. Return the first hit. Keep the name-based path unchanged for backward compatibility. Update the docstring so the model knows both forms are valid.

Do **not** add a separate `find_profile_by_email` tool — a single dual-mode tool is simpler and keeps the skill prompts terse.

### B4. Rewrite the session-start bootstrap

File: [.claude/skills/session-start.md](.claude/skills/session-start.md) (Step 1, lines 17-24; and Step 2 routing)

Replace the current "extract name from user's message" step with this silent flow:

1. **Before reading the user's message**, scan the injected memory/CLAUDE.md context for a `# userEmail` block. If present, call `load_user_profile(email=<that email>)`.
2. **If a profile is returned**:
   - Greet by the profile's `name` field in one short sentence: `"Welcome back, {name} — picking up where we left off."`
   - If the profile has an active workflow, hand off to that skill; otherwise, hand off to `session-router`.
   - Do **not** ask "is that you?" — the email match is authoritative. If the learner says "not me" in their next message, re-prompt with the onboarding fallback below.
3. **If no profile is returned** (or there's no userEmail in context):
   - Derive a display name from the email prefix (`"cyril@yesslab.org"` → `"Cyril"` — capitalized, strip digits/dots). If there is no email at all, use `"learner"`.
   - Hand off directly to `onboarding` with that display name pre-seeded in conversation state.

### B5. Strip the name prompt from onboarding

File: [.claude/skills/onboarding/SKILL.md](.claude/skills/onboarding/SKILL.md)

Currently onboarding greets with `"Hey [name]! I'm SAGE..."` assuming the name was captured upstream. Tighten this:

- Delete any step that asks or implies asking the learner for their name.
- At profile-save time (onboarding Step 5, line 79-84 region), write `email` from session context and `name` as the derived display name. The learner may volunteer a corrected name later in conversation, at which point SAGE updates the profile via `save_user_profile` — but we never **ask**.

File: [data/users/cyril.json](data/users/cyril.json)

Add `"email": "cyril@yesslab.org"` so the memory-context email matches. No other profile needs backfilling — the `practitioner-researcher.json` and other test fixtures are anonymous and stay that way.

### B3. Extend `load_user_profile` to accept `email`

File: [sage/tools.py:36-58](sage/tools.py#L36-L58)

Change the signature from `load_user_profile(name: str)` to `load_user_profile(name: str = "", email: str = "")` with either-or semantics. When `email` is provided, do an exact (case-insensitive) match on the `email` field across all `data/users/*.json` files before falling back to the name-based scan. Return the first hit. Keep the name-based path unchanged for backward compatibility. Update the docstring so the model knows both forms are valid.

Do **not** add a separate `find_profile_by_email` tool — a single dual-mode tool is simpler and keeps the skill prompts terse.

### B4. Rewrite the session-start bootstrap

File: [.claude/skills/session-start.md](.claude/skills/session-start.md) (Step 1, lines 17-24; and Step 2 routing)

Replace the current "extract name from user's message" step with this silent flow:

1. **Before reading the user's message**, scan the injected memory/CLAUDE.md context for a `# userEmail` block. If present, call `load_user_profile(email=<that email>)`.
2. **If a profile is returned**:
   - Greet by the profile's `name` field in one short sentence: `"Welcome back, {name} — picking up where we left off."`
   - If the profile has an active workflow, hand off to that skill; otherwise, hand off to `session-router`.
   - Do **not** ask "is that you?" — the email match is authoritative. If the learner says "not me" in their next message, re-prompt with the onboarding fallback below.
3. **If no profile is returned** (or there's no userEmail in context):
   - Derive a display name from the email prefix (`"cyril@yesslab.org"` → `"Cyril"` — capitalized, strip digits/dots). If there is no email at all, use `"learner"`.
   - Hand off directly to `onboarding` with that display name pre-seeded in conversation state.

### B5. Strip the name prompt from onboarding

File: [.claude/skills/onboarding/SKILL.md](.claude/skills/onboarding/SKILL.md)

Currently onboarding greets with `"Hey [name]! I'm SAGE..."` assuming the name was captured upstream. Tighten this:

- Delete any step that asks or implies asking the learner for their name.
- At profile-save time (onboarding Step 5, line 79-84 region), write `email` from session context and `name` as the derived display name. The learner may volunteer a corrected name later in conversation, at which point SAGE updates the profile via `save_user_profile` — but we never **ask**.

---

## Files to modify

**Part A — Annotated reveal:**
- [data/schemas/scenario.schema.json](data/schemas/scenario.schema.json) — add optional `errors[]` definition under `setup.context`
- [data/scenarios/output-evaluation-errors.json](data/scenarios/output-evaluation-errors.json) — backfill 5 structured error entries
- [.claude/skills/scenario-runner/SKILL.md](.claude/skills/scenario-runner/SKILL.md) — add line-numbered rendering note + Annotated Reveal step + one rule bullet

**Part B — Silent identity matching:**
- [data/schemas/user-profile.schema.json](data/schemas/user-profile.schema.json) — add optional `email` field
- [data/users/cyril.json](data/users/cyril.json) — backfill `"email": "cyril@yesslab.org"`
- [sage/tools.py](sage/tools.py) — extend `load_user_profile` signature to accept `email`
- [.claude/skills/session-start.md](.claude/skills/session-start.md) — replace name-extraction bootstrap with email-first auto-match
- [.claude/skills/onboarding/SKILL.md](.claude/skills/onboarding/SKILL.md) — remove any implied name request; accept a pre-seeded display name

**Shared:**
- [CHANGELOG.md](CHANGELOG.md) — append a dated entry covering both parts (per user memory rule for agent-code edits)

# Plan — Annotated Error Reveal + Silent Identity Matching

## Context

Two improvements to SAGE's CLI learner experience, bundled because both sharpen the first few turns of a practice session.

### Part A — Output Evaluation Annotated Reveal

SAGE's **Output Evaluation** practice type currently presents an AI-generated output with embedded errors as a wall of plain text. The learner identifies errors in free-form conversation, and SAGE explains each error narratively from `groundTruth`. There is no visual scaffolding: no line references, no structured error taxonomy, and no way for SAGE to systematically re-show *where* each error lives after the learner answers.

The user wants to improve this by **revealing errors inline after the learner responds** — an annotated re-display of the AI output where each embedded error is marked with a numbered badge tied to a legend below (type, severity, explanation). This requires a **light schema extension**: an optional `errors[]` array on output-evaluation scenarios so SAGE has structured metadata to anchor the reveal.

Why this matters pedagogically:
- Learners currently finish the practice knowing only whether they "got it right." An annotated reveal lets them see *every* error (including the ones they missed) with precise location and a labeled error type.
- Named types (`hallucination`, `overstatement`, `logical_contradiction`, `missing_context`, `framing`) give learners a transferable vocabulary — one of SAGE's core rules ("Name principles explicitly so learners can transfer them").
- Schema-structured errors also unblock later work (difficulty tuning by severity, progress tracking per error type) without committing to a full taxonomy refactor today.

### Part B — Silent Identity Matching (No Name Prompt)

Today SAGE's session bootstrap ([.claude/skills/session-start.md](.claude/skills/session-start.md)) depends on the learner self-identifying in their first message, and if no name is provided the flow becomes awkward — either onboarding stalls without a display name or the agent has to ask "what should I call you?" This is friction we can eliminate: the Claude Code session already injects `userEmail: cyril@yesslab.org` into the conversation context at start, and a matching `data/users/cyril.json` already exists.

The user wants the agent to **never prompt for a name**. Instead it should: (a) read the email from the memory context, (b) match it to an existing profile, (c) pick up with whatever skill is appropriate — session-router for a returning learner, or onboarding for a first-time one — with onboarding never asking "what's your name?" either. Onboarding derives a display name from the email prefix and lets the learner correct it only if they volunteer.

## Approach

Two independent change sets. Part A is presentation work in scenario-runner; Part B is bootstrap work in session-start + tools.py + schema. They don't share code paths but share a CHANGELOG entry.

---

### Part A — Annotated Error Reveal

Three changes, each small:

### A1. Extend the scenario schema (optional `errors[]`)

File: [data/schemas/scenario.schema.json](data/schemas/scenario.schema.json)

Under `setup.context`, allow the object form to include an `errors` array. Each error entry has:

```json
{
  "id": "e1",
  "quote": "exact substring from aiOutput",
  "lineStart": 2,
  "lineEnd": 2,
  "type": "hallucination",
  "severity": "high",
  "explanation": "Fabricated citation — no Mitchell 2023 study exists."
}
```

- `type` enum: `hallucination`, `overstatement`, `logical_contradiction`, `missing_context`, `bias`, `framing`, `overconfidence`. Reuses three values from the existing `flawType` enum ([scenario.schema.json:65-69](data/schemas/scenario.schema.json#L65-L69)) and adds four output-evaluation-specific types.
- `severity` enum: `low`, `medium`, `high`.
- `lineStart` / `lineEnd` are 1-indexed against a line-split of `aiOutput`.
- `quote` is an exact substring (used as a fallback when line math drifts after editing).
- The whole `errors` array is **optional** — existing `groundTruth`-only scenarios keep working without migration. The schema change is purely additive.

### A2. Backfill the existing scenario

File: [data/scenarios/output-evaluation-errors.json](data/scenarios/output-evaluation-errors.json)

Add an `errors` array under `setup.context` derived from the existing narrative in `groundTruth` ([line 29](data/scenarios/output-evaluation-errors.json#L29)). Five errors to encode:

| id | line(s) | type | severity | quote anchor |
|----|---------|------|----------|--------------|
| e1 | 3 | hallucination | high | "Dr. Sarah Mitchell … Journal of Adolescent Psychology" |
| e2 | 7 | logical_contradiction / overstatement | high | "directly causes depression in teenagers" |
| e3 | 9 | overconfidence | medium | "approximately 90 minutes" |
| e4 | 11 | framing | low | "Instagram was found to be the most harmful" (characterization imprecise) |
| e5 | 13 | missing_context | medium | "adding at least 8 more citations" (compounds fabrication risk) |

Keep `groundTruth` as-is — it stays the narrative companion to the structured list. No pt to delete it.

### A3. Update scenario-runner to render line-numbered output + annotated reveal

File: [.claude/skills/scenario-runner/SKILL.md](.claude/skills/scenario-runner/SKILL.md)

Two insertions, both scoped to OUTPUT EVALUATION only (other practice types unchanged):

**(a) Step 5 — Present output in a line-numbered fenced block** (amend [lines 50-56](.claude/skills/scenario-runner/SKILL.md#L50-L56) with an OUTPUT EVALUATION-specific rendering note):

> For OUTPUT EVALUATION, present `setup.context.aiOutput` as a fenced code block with 1-indexed line numbers, e.g.:
> ````
> ```aioutput
> 1  Here's my feedback on your essay:
> 2
> 3  1. Your thesis about social media increasing anxiety is well-supported. A 2023 study by Dr. Sarah Mitchell in the Journal of Adolescent Psychology found that teens who use social media more than 3 hours daily are 2.4 times more likely to report anxiety symptoms.
> ...
> ```
> ````
> This gives learners a stable reference frame ("line 3 — the Mitchell study…") without changing the source string.

**(b) Step 7 — After EXPLAIN, render the Annotated Reveal** (new sub-step appended after [line 84](.claude/skills/scenario-runner/SKILL.md#L84)):

> If the scenario has `setup.context.errors[]`, close the feedback with an **Annotated Reveal**: a markdown block that re-shows the AI output with `[⚠ E1]`-style inline markers at each error's line, followed by a legend that names the type, severity, and explanation for every error — including the ones the learner already caught and the ones they missed.
>
> Template:
> ```
> ### Annotated reveal
>
> > Line 3: "…Dr. Sarah Mitchell's 2019 study…" [⚠ E1]
> > Line 7: "…directly causes depression in teenagers." [⚠ E2]
>
> **E1** · hallucination · high — Fabricated citation. No such study exists.
> **E2** · overstatement · high — Correlation ≠ causation; causal claim is disputed.
> **E3** · overconfidence · medium — …
> ```
>
> Ordering rule: list what the learner **caught** first (with a brief affirmation), then what they **missed**. This preserves the scaffolding principle — acknowledge before expanding.

**(c) Rules section** ([lines 95-103](.claude/skills/scenario-runner/SKILL.md#L95-L103)): add one bullet — "For OUTPUT EVALUATION scenarios with `errors[]`, end feedback with an Annotated Reveal so every embedded error is named, not just the ones the learner caught."

## Files to modify

- [data/schemas/scenario.schema.json](data/schemas/scenario.schema.json) — add optional `errors[]` definition under `setup.context`
- [data/scenarios/output-evaluation-errors.json](data/scenarios/output-evaluation-errors.json) — backfill 5 structured error entries
- [.claude/skills/scenario-runner/SKILL.md](.claude/skills/scenario-runner/SKILL.md) — add line-numbered rendering note + Annotated Reveal step + one rule bullet
- [CHANGELOG.md](CHANGELOG.md) — append dated entry (per user memory rule for agent-code edits)

## Explicitly out of scope

- Unifying `flawType` (bad-agent-simulator) with the new `errors[].type` enum — would turn this into a "full taxonomy refactor" and was rejected in the scope question.
- Changing the learner's response scaffold. They still respond in free text.
- Any changes to [.claude/skills/skill-evaluator/](.claude/skills/skill-evaluator/) — it continues to score against `evaluationCriteria` as today; it can optionally read `errors[]` later but not now.
- Streamlit/web UI work. CLI markdown only.

## Verification

1. **Schema validity**: `python -c "import json, jsonschema; s=json.load(open('data/schemas/scenario.schema.json')); sc=json.load(open('data/scenarios/output-evaluation-errors.json')); jsonschema.validate(sc, s); print('ok')"` — confirms the backfilled scenario still validates.
2. **Tool round-trip**: invoke `load_scenario` from [sage/tools.py](sage/tools.py) on `scenario-output-evaluation-errors-001` and confirm `errors` is present in the returned object.
3. **End-to-end skill run**: in a fresh Claude Code session, trigger `/scenario-runner` with output evaluation and walk through the scenario. Confirm:
   - The AI output is presented as a fenced code block with line numbers.
   - After the learner's response, the Annotated Reveal block appears with `[⚠ E1]` style markers and a legend listing all 5 errors.
   - Caught errors appear first in the legend with a brief affirmation; missed errors appear after.
   - The closing reflection question (Step 9) still fires.
4. **Backwards compatibility**: re-run any scenario that lacks `errors[]` (none today, but the schema must still accept them) — validate with the same jsonschema call using a stripped-down version of the scenario.

## Explicitly out of scope

**Part A:**
- Unifying `flawType` (bad-agent-simulator) with the new `errors[].type` enum — would turn this into a "full taxonomy refactor" and was rejected in the scope question.
- Changing the learner's response scaffold. They still respond in free text.
- Any changes to [.claude/skills/skill-evaluator/](.claude/skills/skill-evaluator/) — it continues to score against `evaluationCriteria` as today; it can optionally read `errors[]` later but not now.
- Streamlit/web UI work. CLI markdown only.

**Part B:**
- Env-var / git-config identity fallback. Memory-context email is the only input source for now; env vars can be a future fallback if memory is unavailable.
- Migrating existing test-fixture profiles (`practitioner-researcher.json`, `novice-student.json`, etc.) to add email — they stay anonymous.
- Any new `find_profile_by_email` tool. We reuse `load_user_profile` with an optional `email` param.
- Multi-profile-per-email handling. We take the first match; collision handling is a non-issue at current scale.

## Verification

**Part A:**
1. **Schema validity**: `python -c "import json, jsonschema; s=json.load(open('data/schemas/scenario.schema.json')); sc=json.load(open('data/scenarios/output-evaluation-errors.json')); jsonschema.validate(sc, s); print('ok')"` — confirms the backfilled scenario still validates.
2. **Tool round-trip**: invoke `load_scenario` from [sage/tools.py](sage/tools.py) on `scenario-output-evaluation-errors-001` and confirm `errors` is present in the returned object.
3. **End-to-end skill run**: in a fresh Claude Code session, trigger `/scenario-runner` with output evaluation and walk through the scenario. Confirm:
   - The AI output is presented as a fenced code block with line numbers.
   - After the learner's response, the Annotated Reveal block appears with `[⚠ E1]` style markers and a legend listing all 5 errors.
   - Caught errors appear first in the legend with a brief affirmation; missed errors appear after.
   - The closing reflection question (Step 9) still fires.
4. **Backwards compatibility**: validate with the same jsonschema call against a synthetic scenario that omits `errors[]` — the schema must still accept it.

**Part B:**
1. **Profile schema validity**: run jsonschema validation on [data/users/cyril.json](data/users/cyril.json) against the updated user-profile schema. Confirm the new `email` field passes.
2. **Tool round-trip**: in a Python shell, call `load_user_profile(email="cyril@yesslab.org")` and confirm it returns Cyril's profile. Then call `load_user_profile(email="nobody@example.com")` and confirm it returns "not found" without crashing. Finally call `load_user_profile(name="Cyril")` to confirm the legacy path still works.
3. **Bootstrap simulation — returning learner**: start a fresh Claude Code session and type "hi". Confirm SAGE greets with `"Welcome back, Cyril — picking up where we left off."` and routes to session-router **without** asking any identity question.
4. **Bootstrap simulation — unknown learner**: temporarily remove/rename `data/users/cyril.json`, restart the session, type "hi". Confirm SAGE routes straight into onboarding using the derived display name `"Cyril"` (from email prefix) and **never** asks "what's your name?". Restore the file after.
5. **No-regression for non-email sessions**: if `userEmail` is absent from memory (simulate by not injecting it), confirm the old name-extraction path still works — this is the fallback branch in session-start.md.

2. **Tool round-trip**: invoke `load_scenario` from [sage/tools.py](sage/tools.py) on `scenario-output-evaluation-errors-001` and confirm `errors` is present in the returned object.
3. **End-to-end skill run**: in a fresh Claude Code session, trigger `/scenario-runner` with output evaluation and walk through the scenario. Confirm:
   - The AI output is presented as a fenced code block with line numbers.
   - After the learner's response, the Annotated Reveal block appears with `[⚠ E1]` style markers and a legend listing all 5 errors.
   - Caught errors appear first in the legend with a brief affirmation; missed errors appear after.
   - The closing reflection question (Step 9) still fires.
4. **Backwards compatibility**: validate with the same jsonschema call against a synthetic scenario that omits `errors[]` — the schema must still accept it.

**Part B:**
1. **Profile schema validity**: run jsonschema validation on [data/users/cyril.json](data/users/cyril.json) against the updated user-profile schema. Confirm the new `email` field passes.
2. **Tool round-trip**: in a Python shell, call `load_user_profile(email="cyril@yesslab.org")` and confirm it returns Cyril's profile. Then call `load_user_profile(email="nobody@example.com")` and confirm it returns "not found" without crashing. Finally call `load_user_profile(name="Cyril")` to confirm the legacy path still works.
3. **Bootstrap simulation — returning learner**: start a fresh Claude Code session and type "hi". Confirm SAGE greets with `"Welcome back, Cyril — picking up where we left off."` and routes to session-router **without** asking any identity question.
4. **Bootstrap simulation — unknown learner**: temporarily remove/rename `data/users/cyril.json`, restart the session, type "hi". Confirm SAGE routes straight into onboarding using the derived display name `"Cyril"` (from email prefix) and **never** asks "what's your name?". Restore the file after.
5. **No-regression for non-email sessions**: if `userEmail` is absent from memory (simulate by not injecting it), confirm the old name-extraction path still works — this is the fallback branch in session-start.md.

content.content:
1	# Plan — Annotated Error Reveal + Silent Identity Matching
2	
3	## Context
4	
5	Two improvements to SAGE's CLI learner experience, bundled because both sharpen the first few turns of a practice session.
6	
7	### Part A — Output Evaluation Annotated Reveal
8	
9	SAGE's **Output Evaluation** practice type currently presents an AI-generated output with embedded errors as a wall of plain text. The learner identifies errors in free-form conversation, and SAGE explains each error narratively from `groundTruth`. There is no visual scaffolding: no line references, no structured error taxonomy, and no way for SAGE to systematically re-show *where* each error lives after the learner answers.
10	
11	The user wants to improve this by **revealing errors inline after the learner responds** — an annotated re-display of the AI output where each embedded error is marked with a numbered badge tied to a legend below (type, severity, explanation). This requires a **light schema extension**: an optional `errors[]` array on output-evaluation scenarios so SAGE has structured metadata to anchor the reveal.
12	
13	Why this matters pedagogically:
14	- Learners currently finish the practice knowing only whether they "got it right." An annotated reveal lets them see *every* error (including the ones they missed) with precise location and a labeled error type.
15	- Named types (`hallucination`, `overstatement`, `logical_contradiction`, `missing_context`, `framing`) give learners a transferable vocabulary — one of SAGE's core rules ("Name principles explicitly so learners can transfer them").
16	- Schema-structured errors also unblock later work (difficulty tuning by severity, progress tracking per error type) without committing to a full taxonomy refactor today.
17	
18	### Part B — Silent Identity Matching (No Name Prompt)
19	
20	Today SAGE's session bootstrap ([.claude/skills/session-start.md](.claude/skills/session-start.md)) depends on the learner self-identifying in their first message, and if no name is provided the flow becomes awkward — either onboarding stalls without a display name or the agent has to ask "what should I call you?" This is friction we can eliminate: the Claude Code session already injects `userEmail: cyril@yesslab.org` into the conversation context at start, and a matching `data/users/cyril.json` already exists.
21	
22	The user wants the agent to **never prompt for a name**. Instead it should: (a) read the email from the memory context, (b) match it to an existing profile, (c) pick up with whatever skill is appropriate — session-router for a returning learner, or onboarding for a first-time one — with onboarding never asking "what's your name?" either. Onboarding derives a display name from the email prefix and lets the learner correct it only if they volunteer.
23	
24	## Approach
25	
26	Two independent change sets. Part A is presentation work in scenario-runner; Part B is bootstrap work in session-start + tools.py + schema. They don't share code paths but share a CHANGELOG entry.
27	
28	---
29	
30	### Part A — Annotated Error Reveal
31	
32	Three changes, each small:
33	
34	### A1. Extend the scenario schema (optional `errors[]`)
35	
36	File: [data/schemas/scenario.schema.json](data/schemas/scenario.schema.json)
37	
38	Under `setup.context`, allow the object form to include an `errors` array. Each error entry has:
39	
40	```json
41	{
42	  "id": "e1",
43	  "quote": "exact substring from aiOutput",
44	  "lineStart": 2,
45	  "lineEnd": 2,
46	  "type": "hallucination",
47	  "severity": "high",
48	  "explanation": "Fabricated citation — no Mitchell 2023 study exists."
49	}
50	```
51	
52	- `type` enum: `hallucination`, `overstatement`, `logical_contradiction`, `missing_context`, `bias`, `framing`, `overconfidence`. Reuses three values from the existing `flawType` enum ([scenario.schema.json:65-69](data/schemas/scenario.schema.json#L65-L69)) and adds four output-evaluation-specific types.
53	- `severity` enum: `low`, `medium`, `high`.
54	- `lineStart` / `lineEnd` are 1-indexed against a line-split of `aiOutput`.
55	- `quote` is an exact substring (used as a fallback when line math drifts after editing).
56	- The whole `errors` array is **optional** — existing `groundTruth`-only scenarios keep working without migration. The schema change is purely additive.
57	
58	### A2. Backfill the existing scenario
59	
60	File: [data/scenarios/output-evaluation-errors.json](data/scenarios/output-evaluation-errors.json)
61	
62	Add an `errors` array under `setup.context` derived from the existing narrative in `groundTruth` ([line 29](data/scenarios/output-evaluation-errors.json#L29)). Five errors to encode:
63	
64	| id | line(s) | type | severity | quote anchor |
65	|----|---------|------|----------|--------------|
66	| e1 | 3 | hallucination | high | "Dr. Sarah Mitchell … Journal of Adolescent Psychology" |
67	| e2 | 7 | logical_contradiction / overstatement | high | "directly causes depression in teenagers" |
68	| e3 | 9 | overconfidence | medium | "approximately 90 minutes" |
69	| e4 | 11 | framing | low | "Instagram was found to be the most harmful" (characterization imprecise) |
70	| e5 | 13 | missing_context | medium | "adding at least 8 more citations" (compounds fabrication risk) |
71	
72	Keep `groundTruth` as-is — it stays the narrative companion to the structured list. No pt to delete it.
73	
74	### A3. Update scenario-runner to render line-numbered output + annotated reveal
75	
76	File: [.claude/skills/scenario-runner/SKILL.md](.claude/skills/scenario-runner/SKILL.md)
77	
78	Two insertions, both scoped to OUTPUT EVALUATION only (other practice types unchanged):
79	
80	**(a) Step 5 — Present output in a line-numbered fenced block** (amend [lines 50-56](.claude/skills/scenario-runner/SKILL.md#L50-L56) with an OUTPUT EVALUATION-specific rendering note):
81	
82	> For OUTPUT EVALUATION, present `setup.context.aiOutput` as a fenced code block with 1-indexed line numbers, e.g.:
83	> ````
84	> ```aioutput
85	> 1  Here's my feedback on your essay:
86	> 2
87	> 3  1. Your thesis about social media increasing anxiety is well-supported. A 2023 study by Dr. Sarah Mitchell in the Journal of Adolescent Psychology found that teens who use social media more than 3 hours daily are 2.4 times more likely to report anxiety symptoms.
88	> ...
89	> ```
90	> ````
91	> This gives learners a stable reference frame ("line 3 — the Mitchell study…") without changing the source string.
92	
93	**(b) Step 7 — After EXPLAIN, render the Annotated Reveal** (new sub-step appended after [line 84](.claude/skills/scenario-runner/SKILL.md#L84)):
94	
95	> If the scenario has `setup.context.errors[]`, close the feedback with an **Annotated Reveal**: a markdown block that re-shows the AI output with `[⚠ E1]`-style inline markers at each error's line, followed by a legend that names the type, severity, and explanation for every error — including the ones the learner already caught and the ones they missed.
96	>
97	> Template:
98	> ```
99	> ### Annotated reveal
100	>
101	> > Line 3: "…Dr. Sarah Mitchell's 2019 study…" [⚠ E1]
102	> > Line 7: "…directly causes depression in teenagers." [⚠ E2]
103	>
104	> **E1** · hallucination · high — Fabricated citation. No such study exists.
105	> **E2** · overstatement · high — Correlation ≠ causation; causal claim is disputed.
106	> **E3** · overconfidence · medium — …
107	> ```
108	>
109	> Ordering rule: list what the learner **caught** first (with a brief affirmation), then what they **missed**. This preserves the scaffolding principle — acknowledge before expanding.
110	
111	**(c) Rules section** ([lines 95-103](.claude/skills/scenario-runner/SKILL.md#L95-L103)): add one bullet — "For OUTPUT EVALUATION scenarios with `errors[]`, end feedback with an Annotated Reveal so every embedded error is named, not just the ones the learner caught."
112	
113	---
114	
115	### Part B — Silent Identity Matching
116	
117	Four small changes. The bootstrap flow becomes: read `userEmail` from injected memory context → try `load_user_profile(email=...)` → if hit, route straight to session-router / resume; if miss, route straight to onboarding without asking for a name.
118	
119	### B1. Add `email` to the user-profile schema
120	
121	File: [data/schemas/user-profile.schema.json](data/schemas/user-profile.schema.json)
122	
123	Add an optional `email` string field under `properties`. Do **not** add it to `required` — existing profiles without email keep working. This field is the anchor for auto-match.
124	
125	### B2. Backfill the existing Cyril profile with email
126	
127	File: [data/users/cyril.json](data/users/cyril.json)
128	
129	Add `"email": "cyril@yesslab.org"` so the memory-context email matches. No other profile needs backfilling — the `practitioner-researcher.json` and other test fixtures are anonymous and stay that way.
130	
131	### B3. Extend `load_user_profile` to accept `email`
132	
133	File: [sage/tools.py:36-58](sage/tools.py#L36-L58)
134	
135	Change the signature from `load_user_profile(name: str)` to `load_user_profile(name: str = "", email: str = "")` with either-or semantics. When `email` is provided, do an exact (case-insensitive) match on the `email` field across all `data/users/*.json` files before falling back to the name-based scan. Return the first hit. Keep the name-based path unchanged for backward compatibility. Update the docstring so the model knows both forms are valid.
136	
137	Do **not** add a separate `find_profile_by_email` tool — a single dual-mode tool is simpler and keeps the skill prompts terse.
138	
139	### B4. Rewrite the session-start bootstrap
140	
141	File: [.claude/skills/session-start.md](.claude/skills/session-start.md) (Step 1, lines 17-24; and Step 2 routing)
142	
143	Replace the current "extract name from user's message" step with this silent flow:
144	
145	1. **Before reading the user's message**, scan the injected memory/CLAUDE.md context for a `# userEmail` block. If present, call `load_user_profile(email=<that email>)`.
146	2. **If a profile is returned**:
147	   - Greet by the profile's `name` field in one short sentence: `"Welcome back, {name} — picking up where we left off."`
148	   - If the profile has an active workflow, hand off to that skill; otherwise, hand off to `session-router`.
149	   - Do **not** ask "is that you?" — the email match is authoritative. If the learner says "not me" in their next message, re-prompt with the onboarding fallback below.
150	3. **If no profile is returned** (or there's no userEmail in context):
151	   - Derive a display name from the email prefix (`"cyril@yesslab.org"` → `"Cyril"` — capitalized, strip digits/dots). If there is no email at all, use `"learner"`.
152	   - Hand off directly to `onboarding` with that display name pre-seeded in conversation state.
153	
154	### B5. Strip the name prompt from onboarding
155	
156	File: [.claude/skills/onboarding/SKILL.md](.claude/skills/onboarding/SKILL.md)
157	
158	Currently onboarding greets with `"Hey [name]! I'm SAGE..."` assuming the name was captured upstream. Tighten this:
159	
160	- Delete any step that asks or implies asking the learner for their name.
161	- At profile-save time (onboarding Step 5, line 79-84 region), write `email` from session context and `name` as the derived display name. The learner may volunteer a corrected name later in conversation, at which point SAGE updates the profile via `save_user_profile` — but we never **ask**.
162	
163	---
164	
165	## Files to modify
166	
167	**Part A — Annotated reveal:**
168	- [data/schemas/scenario.schema.json](data/schemas/scenario.schema.json) — add optional `errors[]` definition under `setup.context`
169	- [data/scenarios/output-evaluation-errors.json](data/scenarios/output-evaluation-errors.json) — backfill 5 structured error entries
170	- [.claude/skills/scenario-runner/SKILL.md](.claude/skills/scenario-runner/SKILL.md) — add line-numbered rendering note + Annotated Reveal step + one rule bullet
171	
172	**Part B — Silent identity matching:**
173	- [data/schemas/user-profile.schema.json](data/schemas/user-profile.schema.json) — add optional `email` field
174	- [data/users/cyril.json](data/users/cyril.json) — backfill `"email": "cyril@yesslab.org"`
175	- [sage/tools.py](sage/tools.py) — extend `load_user_profile` signature to accept `email`
176	- [.claude/skills/session-start.md](.claude/skills/session-start.md) — replace name-extraction bootstrap with email-first auto-match
177	- [.claude/skills/onboarding/SKILL.md](.claude/skills/onboarding/SKILL.md) — remove any implied name request; accept a pre-seeded display name
178	
179	**Shared:**
180	- [CHANGELOG.md](CHANGELOG.md) — append a dated entry covering both parts (per user memory rule for agent-code edits)
181	
182	## Explicitly out of scope
183	
184	**Part A:**
185	- Unifying `flawType` (bad-agent-simulator) with the new `errors[].type` enum — would turn this into a "full taxonomy refactor" and was rejected in the scope question.
186	- Changing the learner's response scaffold. They still respond in free text.

192	- Migrating existing test-fixture profiles (`practitioner-researcher.json`, `novice-student.json`, etc.) to add email — they stay anonymous.
193	- Any new `find_profile_by_email` tool. We reuse `load_user_profile` with an optional `email` param.

# Plan — Annotated Error Reveal + Silent Identity Matching

## Context

Two improvements to SAGE's CLI learner experience, bundled because both sharpen the first few turns of a practice session.

### Part A — Output Evaluation Annotated Reveal

SAGE's **Output Evaluation** practice type currently presents an AI-generated output with embedded errors as a wall of plain text. The learner identifies errors in free-form conversation, and SAGE explains each error narratively from `groundTruth`. There is no visual scaffolding: no line references, no structured error taxonomy, and no way for SAGE to systematically re-show *where* each error lives after the learner answers.

The user wants to improve this by **revealing errors inline after the learner responds** — an annotated re-display of the AI output where each embedded error is marked with a numbered badge tied to a legend below (type, severity, explanation). This requires a **light schema extension**: an optional `errors[]` array on output-evaluation scenarios so SAGE has structured metadata to anchor the reveal.

Why this matters pedagogically:
- Learners currently finish the practice knowing only whether they "got it right." An annotated reveal lets them see *every* error (including the ones they missed) with precise location and a labeled error type.
- Named types (`hallucination`, `overstatement`, `logical_contradiction`, `missing_context`, `framing`) give learners a transferable vocabulary — one of SAGE's core rules ("Name principles explicitly so learners can transfer them").
- Schema-structured errors also unblock later work (difficulty tuning by severity, progress tracking per error type) without committing to a full taxonomy refactor today.

### Part B — Silent Identity Matching (No Name Prompt)

Today SAGE's session bootstrap ([.claude/skills/session-start.md](.claude/skills/session-start.md)) depends on the learner self-identifying in their first message, and if no name is provided the flow becomes awkward — either onboarding stalls without a display name or the agent has to ask "what should I call you?" This is friction we can eliminate: the Claude Code session already injects `userEmail: cyril@yesslab.org` into the conversation context at start, and a matching `data/users/cyril.json` already exists.

The user wants the agent to **never prompt for a name**. Instead it should: (a) read the email from the memory context, (b) match it to an existing profile, (c) pick up with whatever skill is appropriate — session-router for a returning learner, or onboarding for a first-time one — with onboarding never asking "what's your name?" either. Onboarding derives a display name from the email prefix and lets the learner correct it only if they volunteer.

## Approach

Two independent change sets. Part A is presentation work in scenario-runner; Part B is bootstrap work in session-start + tools.py + schema. They don't share code paths but share a CHANGELOG entry.

---

### Part A — Annotated Error Reveal

Three changes, each small:

### A1. Extend the scenario schema (optional `errors[]`)

File: [data/schemas/scenario.schema.json](data/schemas/scenario.schema.json)

Under `setup.context`, allow the object form to include an `errors` array. Each error entry has:

```json
{
  "id": "e1",
  "quote": "exact substring from aiOutput",
  "lineStart": 2,
  "lineEnd": 2,
  "type": "hallucination",
  "severity": "high",
  "explanation": "Fabricated citation — no Mitchell 2023 study exists."
}
```

- `type` enum: `hallucination`, `overstatement`, `logical_contradiction`, `missing_context`, `bias`, `framing`, `overconfidence`. Reuses three values from the existing `flawType` enum ([scenario.schema.json:65-69](data/schemas/scenario.schema.json#L65-L69)) and adds four output-evaluation-specific types.
- `severity` enum: `low`, `medium`, `high`.
- `lineStart` / `lineEnd` are 1-indexed against a line-split of `aiOutput`.
- `quote` is an exact substring (used as a fallback when line math drifts after editing).
- The whole `errors` array is **optional** — existing `groundTruth`-only scenarios keep working without migration. The schema change is purely additive.

### A2. Backfill the existing scenario

File: [data/scenarios/output-evaluation-errors.json](data/scenarios/output-evaluation-errors.json)

Add an `errors` array under `setup.context` derived from the existing narrative in `groundTruth` ([line 29](data/scenarios/output-evaluation-errors.json#L29)). Five errors to encode:

| id | line(s) | type | severity | quote anchor |
|----|---------|------|----------|--------------|
| e1 | 3 | hallucination | high | "Dr. Sarah Mitchell … Journal of Adolescent Psychology" |
| e2 | 7 | logical_contradiction / overstatement | high | "directly causes depression in teenagers" |
| e3 | 9 | overconfidence | medium | "approximately 90 minutes" |
| e4 | 11 | framing | low | "Instagram was found to be the most harmful" (characterization imprecise) |
| e5 | 13 | missing_context | medium | "adding at least 8 more citations" (compounds fabrication risk) |

Keep `groundTruth` as-is — it stays the narrative companion to the structured list. No pt to delete it.

### A3. Update scenario-runner to render line-numbered output + annotated reveal

File: [.claude/skills/scenario-runner/SKILL.md](.claude/skills/scenario-runner/SKILL.md)

Two insertions, both scoped to OUTPUT EVALUATION only (other practice types unchanged):

**(a) Step 5 — Present output in a line-numbered fenced block** (amend [lines 50-56](.claude/skills/scenario-runner/SKILL.md#L50-L56) with an OUTPUT EVALUATION-specific rendering note):

> For OUTPUT EVALUATION, present `setup.context.aiOutput` as a fenced code block with 1-indexed line numbers, e.g.:
> ````
> ```aioutput
> 1  Here's my feedback on your essay:
> 2
> 3  1. Your thesis about social media increasing anxiety is well-supported. A 2023 study by Dr. Sarah Mitchell in the Journal of Adolescent Psychology found that teens who use social media more than 3 hours daily are 2.4 times more likely to report anxiety symptoms.
> ...
> ```
> ````
> This gives learners a stable reference frame ("line 3 — the Mitchell study…") without changing the source string.

**(b) Step 7 — After EXPLAIN, render the Annotated Reveal** (new sub-step appended after [line 84](.claude/skills/scenario-runner/SKILL.md#L84)):

> If the scenario has `setup.context.errors[]`, close the feedback with an **Annotated Reveal**: a markdown block that re-shows the AI output with `[⚠ E1]`-style inline markers at each error's line, followed by a legend that names the type, severity, and explanation for every error — including the ones the learner already caught and the ones they missed.
>
> Template:
> ```
> ### Annotated reveal
>
> > Line 3: "…Dr. Sarah Mitchell's 2019 study…" [⚠ E1]
> > Line 7: "…directly causes depression in teenagers." [⚠ E2]
>
> **E1** · hallucination · high — Fabricated citation. No such study exists.
> **E2** · overstatement · high — Correlation ≠ causation; causal claim is disputed.
> **E3** · overconfidence · medium — …
> ```
>
> Ordering rule: list what the learner **caught** first (with a brief affirmation), then what they **missed**. This preserves the scaffolding principle — acknowledge before expanding.

**(c) Rules section** ([lines 95-103](.claude/skills/scenario-runner/SKILL.md#L95-L103)): add one bullet — "For OUTPUT EVALUATION scenarios with `errors[]`, end feedback with an Annotated Reveal so every embedded error is named, not just the ones the learner caught."

---

### Part B — Silent Identity Matching

Four small changes. The bootstrap flow becomes: read `userEmail` from injected memory context → try `load_user_profile(email=...)` → if hit, route straight to session-router / resume; if miss, route straight to onboarding without asking for a name.

### B1. Add `email` to the user-profile schema

File: [data/schemas/user-profile.schema.json](data/schemas/user-profile.schema.json)

Add an optional `email` string field under `properties`. Do **not** add it to `required` — existing profiles without email keep working. This field is the anchor for auto-match.

### B2. Backfill the existing Cyril profile with email

File: [data/users/cyril.json](data/users/cyril.json)

Add `"email": "cyril@yesslab.org"` so the memory-context email matches. No other profile needs backfilling — the `practitioner-researcher.json` and other test fixtures are anonymous and stay that way.

### B3. Extend `load_user_profile` to accept `email`

File: [sage/tools.py:36-58](sage/tools.py#L36-L58)

Change the signature from `load_user_profile(name: str)` to `load_user_profile(name: str = "", email: str = "")` with either-or semantics. When `email` is provided, do an exact (case-insensitive) match on the `email` field across all `data/users/*.json` files before falling back to the name-based scan. Return the first hit. Keep the name-based path unchanged for backward compatibility. Update the docstring so the model knows both forms are valid.

Do **not** add a separate `find_profile_by_email` tool — a single dual-mode tool is simpler and keeps the skill prompts terse.

### B4. Rewrite the session-start bootstrap

File: [.claude/skills/session-start.md](.claude/skills/session-start.md) (Step 1, lines 17-24; and Step 2 routing)

Replace the current "extract name from user's message" step with this silent flow:

1. **Before reading the user's message**, scan the injected memory/CLAUDE.md context for a `# userEmail` block. If present, call `load_user_profile(email=<that email>)`.
2. **If a profile is returned**:
   - Greet by the profile's `name` field in one short sentence: `"Welcome back, {name} — picking up where we left off."`
   - If the profile has an active workflow, hand off to that skill; otherwise, hand off to `session-router`.
   - Do **not** ask "is that you?" — the email match is authoritative. If the learner says "not me" in their next message, re-prompt with the onboarding fallback below.
3. **If no profile is returned** (or there's no userEmail in context):
   - Derive a display name from the email prefix (`"cyril@yesslab.org"` → `"Cyril"` — capitalized, strip digits/dots). If there is no email at all, use `"learner"`.
   - Hand off directly to `onboarding` with that display name pre-seeded in conversation state.

### B5. Strip the name prompt from onboarding

File: [.claude/skills/onboarding/SKILL.md](.claude/skills/onboarding/SKILL.md)

Currently onboarding greets with `"Hey [name]! I'm SAGE..."` assuming the name was captured upstream. Tighten this:

- Delete any step that asks or implies asking the learner for their name.
- At profile-save time (onboarding Step 5, line 79-84 region), write `email` from session context and `name` as the derived display name. The learner may volunteer a corrected name later in conversation, at which point SAGE updates the profile via `save_user_profile` — but we never **ask**.

---

- [data/scenarios/output-evaluation-errors.json](data/scenarios/output-evaluation-errors.json) — backfill 5 structured error entries
- [.claude/skills/scenario-runner/SKILL.md](.claude/skills/scenario-runner/SKILL.md) — add line-numbered rendering note + Annotated Reveal step + one rule bullet

- [data/users/cyril.json](data/users/cyril.json) — backfill `"email": "cyril@yesslab.org"`
- [sage/tools.py](sage/tools.py) — extend `load_user_profile` signature to accept `email`
- [.claude/skills/session-start.md](.claude/skills/session-start.md) — replace name-extraction bootstrap with email-first auto-match
- [.claude/skills/onboarding/SKILL.md](.claude/skills/onboarding/SKILL.md) — remove any implied name request; accept a pre-seeded display name

**Shared:**
- [CHANGELOG.md](CHANGELOG.md) — append a dated entry covering both parts (per user memory rule for agent-code edits)

## Explicitly out of scope

**Part A:**
- Unifying `flawType` (bad-agent-simulator) with the new `errors[].type` enum — would turn this into a "full taxonomy refactor" and was rejected in the scope question.
- Changing the learner's response scaffold. They still respond in free text.
- Any changes to [.claude/skills/skill-evaluator/](.claude/skills/skill-evaluator/) — it continues to score against `evaluationCriteria` as today; it can optionally read `errors[]` later but not now.
- Streamlit/web UI work. CLI markdown only.

**Part B:**

- Migrating existing test-fixture profiles (`practitioner-researcher.json`, `novice-student.json`, etc.) to add email — they stay anonymous.
- Any new `find_profile_by_email` tool. We reuse `load_user_profile` with an optional `email` param.
- Multi-profile-per-email handling. We take the first match; collision handling is a non-issue at current scale.

## Verification

**Part A:**
1. **Schema validity**: `python -c "import json, jsonschema; s=json.load(open('data/schemas/scenario.schema.json')); sc=json.load(open('data/scenarios/output-evaluation-errors.json')); jsonschema.validate(sc, s); print('ok')"` — confirms the backfilled scenario still validates.
2. **Tool round-trip**: invoke `load_scenario` from [sage/tools.py](sage/tools.py) on `scenario-output-evaluation-errors-001` and confirm `errors` is present in the returned object.

   - The AI output is presented as a fenced code block with line numbers.

   - Caught errors appear first in the legend with a brief affirmation; missed errors appear after.
   - The closing reflection question (Step 9) still fires.
4. **Backwards compatibility**: validate with the same jsonschema call against a synthetic scenario that omits `errors[]` — the schema must still accept it.

**Part B:**
1. **Profile schema validity**: run jsonschema validation on [data/users/cyril.json](data/users/cyril.json) against the updated user-profile schema. Confirm the new `email` field passes.

Then call `load_user_profile(email="nobody@example.com")` and confirm it returns "not found" without crashing. Finally call `load_user_profile(name="Cyril")` to confirm the legacy path still works.

# Plan — Annotated Error Reveal + Silent Identity Matching

## Context

Two improvements to SAGE's CLI learner experience, bundled because both sharpen the first few turns of a practice session.

### Part A — Output Evaluation Annotated Reveal

SAGE's **Output Evaluation** practice type currently presents an AI-generated output with embedded errors as a wall of plain text. The learner identifies errors in free-form conversation, and SAGE explains each error narratively from `groundTruth`. There is no visual scaffolding: no line references, no structured error taxonomy, and no way for SAGE to systematically re-show *where* each error lives after the learner answers.

The user wants to improve this by **revealing errors inline after the learner responds** — an annotated re-display of the AI output where each embedded error is marked with a numbered badge tied to a legend below (type, severity, explanation). This requires a **light schema extension**: an optional `errors[]` array on output-evaluation scenarios so SAGE has structured metadata to anchor the reveal.

Why this matters pedagogically:
- Learners currently finish the practice knowing only whether they "got it right." An annotated reveal lets them see *every* error (including the ones they missed) with precise location and a labeled error type.
- Named types (`hallucination`, `overstatement`, `logical_contradiction`, `missing_context`, `framing`) give learners a transferable vocabulary — one of SAGE's core rules ("Name principles explicitly so learners can transfer them").
- Schema-structured errors also unblock later work (difficulty tuning by severity, progress tracking per error type) without committing to a full taxonomy refactor today.

### Part B — Silent Identity Matching (No Name Prompt)

Today SAGE's session bootstrap ([.claude/skills/session-start.md](.claude/skills/session-start.md)) depends on the learner self-identifying in their first message, and if no name is provided the flow becomes awkward — either onboarding stalls without a display name or the agent has to ask "what should I call you?" This is friction we can eliminate: the Claude Code session already injects `userEmail: cyril@yesslab.org` into the conversation context at start, and a matching `data/users/cyril.json` already exists.

The user wants the agent to **never prompt for a name**. Instead it should: (a) read the email from the memory context, (b) match it to an existing profile, (c) pick up with whatever skill is appropriate — session-router for a returning learner, or onboarding for a first-time one — with onboarding never asking "what's your name?" either. Onboarding derives a display name from the email prefix and lets the learner correct it only if they volunteer.

## Approach

Two independent change sets. Part A is presentation work in scenario-runner; Part B is bootstrap work in session-start + tools.py + schema. They don't share code paths but share a CHANGELOG entry.

---

### Part A — Annotated Error Reveal

Three changes, each small:

### A1. Extend the scenario schema (optional `errors[]`)

File: [data/schemas/scenario.schema.json](data/schemas/scenario.schema.json)

Under `setup.context`, allow the object form to include an `errors` array. Each error entry has:

```json
{
  "id": "e1",
  "quote": "exact substring from aiOutput",
  "lineStart": 2,
  "lineEnd": 2,
  "type": "hallucination",
  "severity": "high",
  "explanation": "Fabricated citation — no Mitchell 2023 study exists."
}
```

- `type` enum: `hallucination`, `overstatement`, `logical_contradiction`, `missing_context`, `bias`, `framing`, `overconfidence`. Reuses three values from the existing `flawType` enum ([scenario.schema.json:65-69](data/schemas/scenario.schema.json#L65-L69)) and adds four output-evaluation-specific types.
- `severity` enum: `low`, `medium`, `high`.
- `lineStart` / `lineEnd` are 1-indexed against a line-split of `aiOutput`.
- `quote` is an exact substring (used as a fallback when line math drifts after editing).
- The whole `errors` array is **optional** — existing `groundTruth`-only scenarios keep working without migration. The schema change is purely additive.

### A2. Backfill the existing scenario

File: [data/scenarios/output-evaluation-errors.json](data/scenarios/output-evaluation-errors.json)

Add an `errors` array under `setup.context` derived from the existing narrative in `groundTruth` ([line 29](data/scenarios/output-evaluation-errors.json#L29)). Five errors to encode:

| id | line(s) | type | severity | quote anchor |
|----|---------|------|----------|--------------|
| e1 | 3 | hallucination | high | "Dr. Sarah Mitchell … Journal of Adolescent Psychology" |
| e2 | 7 | logical_contradiction / overstatement | high | "directly causes depression in teenagers" |
| e3 | 9 | overconfidence | medium | "approximately 90 minutes" |
| e4 | 11 | framing | low | "Instagram was found to be the most harmful" (characterization imprecise) |
| e5 | 13 | missing_context | medium | "adding at least 8 more citations" (compounds fabrication risk) |

Keep `groundTruth` as-is — it stays the narrative companion to the structured list. No need to delete it.

### A3. Update scenario-runner to render line-numbered output + annotated reveal

File: [.claude/skills/scenario-runner/SKILL.md](.claude/skills/scenario-runner/SKILL.md)

Two insertions, both scoped to OUTPUT EVALUATION only (other practice types unchanged):

**(a) Step 5 — Present output in a line-numbered fenced block** (amend [lines 50-56](.claude/skills/scenario-runner/SKILL.md#L50-L56) with an OUTPUT EVALUATION-specific rendering note):

> This gives learners a stable reference frame ("line 3 — the Mitchell study…") without changing the source string.

>

> ### Annotated reveal
>
> > Line 3: "…Dr. Sarah Mitchell's 2019 study…" [⚠ E1]

>
> **E1** · hallucination · high — Fabricated citation. No such study exists.
> **E2** · overstatement · high — Correlation ≠ causation; causal claim is disputed.
> **E3** · overconfidence · medium — …

>
> Ordering rule: list what the learner **caught** first (with a brief affirmation), then what they **missed**. This preserves the scaffolding principle — acknowledge before expanding.

**(c) Rules section** ([lines 95-103](.claude/skills/scenario-runner/SKILL.md#L95-L103)): add one bullet — "For OUTPUT EVALUATION scenarios with `errors[]`, end feedback with an Annotated Reveal so every embedded error is named, not just the ones the learner caught."
